titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Jim Dine Papers
titlestmt.author: Elise Riley

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday February 27, 2026 at 23:25

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 9 (born digital): Restricted fragile. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Jim Dine in honor of Daniel Clark (Yale 1993), 2025.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is organized into two series: I. Poems, 2003-2024. II. Computer media, circa 2020.

bioghist.head: Jim Dine (1935 - )
bioghist.p: Jim Dine (1935- ) is an American artist whose career spans over sixty years. Born in Ohio, Dine studied at the University of Cincinnati, the Boston Museum school, and earned a BFA from Ohio University in 1957. While at school Dine experimented with several types of artistic practices including lithography, etching, intaglio, and woodcuts. In 1958 he moved to New York City where he founded the Judson Gallery with Claes Oldenburg and others who became known for their performance art or "Happenings."

Dine's work is multimedia and extends to painting, drawing, printmaking, sculpture, photography, poetry, and performance. His work is associated with the Pop Art, Neo-Dada, and Neo-Expressionist art movements. Since the 1970s, Dine's art has been the subject of over 300 solo exhibitions and can be found in public collections around the world at institutions such as MoMA, Tate Gallery, Tokyo Metropolitan Art Museum, Centre Goerges Pompidou, and many others. He has also guest lectured at several universities including Zurich University of the Arts and Yale University.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Jim Dine papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Yale Archival Processing Manual.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection contains poems, manuscripts, writings, notes, books, born-digital material, and other papers created by or related to Jim Dine.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Jim Dine papers is the physical property of the Beinecke, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains the subject files for poems created by Jim Dine. The folders contain manuscript and typescript drafts, poems, writings, notes, and print material.

Drafts of poems were written with a range of materials including paint, charcoal, and ink on a mix of paper types. Dine often drafted on long rolls of paper taped together, in the margins of books, or on the back of paper bags. Also included with the drafts are copies of the book where the poem was published.

did.unittitle: Korea (magic on paper collages) and "The Gypsy Painter"
"Windsor and Newton Tests on the Vellum"
"Seeing Thru the Stardust, The Heat on the Lawn (Cloude)"
La Vie La Coupole and Poems to Work On
